inline_comments.message: suggestion: remove these methods and just add the parameters to the getInstance() call
wrap in try-with-resources so that it will be automatically closed

if we know this is always needed, then this is ok.  If not, then it should be done on-demand instead.  In that case, the event manager should cache it internally and whenever getCqResponse() is called, would query once and return the cached value thereafter.

won't this throw NPE if AAI_CUSTOM_QUERY is false?

same comment below.

Hmm, looks like several actors were invoking getCqResponse() rather than using the cached response.  Wondering if they all really need the custom query? (i.e., What should they do if AAI_CUSTOM_QUERY is false? Do the custom query anyway?)
wrap in try-with-resources so the input stream is closed

remove trailing spaces

same comment through-out this file
looks like these properties should be moved to the top level pom (or to parent/pom)
since this is "provided", is it necessary to specify the version? (just wondering)
allocate the port via NetworkUtil.allocPort() instead of using a hard-coded port
remove the try-catch wrapper
remove trailing spaces
remove the comment

If AAI_CUSTOM_QUERY is false this block will not be executed. This appears to me equivalent to  current version flow.

If AAI_CUSTOM_QUERY is false custom query is not executed.

I think that adding a complete caching system for aai requests is in the plan, should be in the backlog. Then, implementing aai caching is not in the scope of this change.
Listener is now added to its own client instance.

It you look at the other *.RESPONSE rules, the retracting of objects is done a bit differently, it is in order to respect the retry operations from the actual operational policy.   I think you would need to do something similar if the same parameters in the policy are to be applied.

This would also imply that would need to take care of response objects and have clean up rules.

Not a comment but another way to do this through PolicyEngine directly:

https://git.onap.org/policy/drools-applications/tree/controlloop/common/feature-controlloop-management/src/test/java/org/onap/policy/drools/server/restful/RestControlLoopManagerTest.java

It was provided initially and removed in patch 9. I don't have all the details in mind but it was related to integration testing fixes.

Caching mechanism is not in place yet. From my understanding this is a feature still to come from the backlog. 

During October 1st meeting, we decided to move the custom query call here to be more efficient and avoid making the same call twice.

Yes it should and it was initially and remove in patch 7 because of a cds bug with the parent (CCSDK-1795).
I'm suggesting to create a jira to move this ccsdk dependency in the parent and to complete it in a separate change.
Done
Done
the other rules in this file appear to use $params.getPolicyScope().  Perhaps the test case needs some enhancement.

Note that usecases and amsterdam drl are pretty similar but differ on the use of ${.}, may be you're applying the changes to "amsterdam" drl instead of this one.   Just to clarify, these changes are just particular to usecases, not to amsterdam.   I know it is confusing but the amsterdam will be eventually soon be deprecated and removed from codebase, it is just there to support the old architecture.

Moving the call to AAI during the instantiation of ControlLoopManager, will mean that the call will be made only once, when the manager is instantiated. In the current implementation, for the other use cases, the AaiCqResponse value should get overwritten when other actors (use cases) are invoked, when they make this AAI call separately . So I am guessing it should work okay. 
But whether or not to use this as a generic solution (and remove the subsequent calls) for all other use cases will depend on how often the Manager gets instantiated. That will need discussion.

Done.

Exact, I was making the change for amsterdam and once tested successfully I applied the exact same change to usecases.

Now amsterdam is using ${policyScope} and usecases is using $params.getPolicyScope().

My only concern is that we were not really able to test usecases.
need to close the session once you have the response right?
maybe log this?
cleanup?

nitpick: since you are already loading the yaml above, you can insert these into the YAML file instead of setting it on policy. Sample:

policies:
  - id: unique-policy-id-1-restart
    name: Restart Policy
    description:
    actor: CDS
    recipe: Restart
    target:
      resourceID: bbb3cefd-01c8-413c-9bdd-2b92f9ca3d38
      type: VNF
    payload:
      artifact_name: my-artifact-name
      artifact_version: 1.0.0
    retry: 1
    timeout: 20
    success: final_success
    failure: unique-policy-id-2-rebuild
    failure_timeout: unique-policy-id-2-rebuild
    failure_retries: unique-policy-id-2-rebuild
    failure_exception: final_failure_exception

please refer to the previous comment. Maybe rename to modifyConfig to match the usecase we are testing. Also the below two items using actor APPC can be removed for sake of clarity since they are not being used in the unit test

I am not sure I understand the reason you set the custom query to false in this use case?

I reviewed the current flow for this test in master branch and saw that custom query was not executed. Then, the flag was set to false here to ensure that the flow remains identical and custom query is still not executed with the change we are making.
Pramod can fix in his review - as he will be removing that flag to ensure all tests etc. use the Custom Query.

Remember to add CDS_GRPC* installation variables in the following repos/paths:

policy/docker/*/drools/base.conf
oom/kubernetes/policy/charts/drools/*/base.conf
